speaker: interviewer
text: Adler. It is March 3, 1996. So Mrs. Adler, you were describing various run-ins, or little situations that you had while you were working at this factory. Is there anything else from this time period before you and your family were deported to Theresienstadt that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not off-hand, no. Not off-hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you-- when did you first find out that you were being deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the first time I found out that I was supposed to be deported was in '41. I was supposed to go to Lodz. And then, since my mother was sick, and she could not be transported, and they didn't want me to go alone, I was taken out of that And then, I think, periodically, for the next few times, I was in and out. In transport, I got the papers. And then I tried to get myself out again. Myself, because I figured, it's easier to do it myself than have the grown-ups involved. Then it gets very complicated. You know, with your own two feet and your own mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So deportations from Hamburg began in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41. No, I mean, there were some before, but they went to Poland. Those were non-German people, so they went before. But I shouldn't say that, that they started in '41. But the Polish individuals had to lave earlier, but I don't know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were people living in Germany with Polish citizenship or of Polish descent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some of your friends among the deportees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, sure-- many of them. People I met in gardening school, people I met in sewing school, and people you knew. It was very painful-- very painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of these deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would take place? Describe it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people had to be-- were collected in a certain place the evening before. And then they were fed with some kind of food. I don't know, whatever it was. And then they were hauled into, like, trucks, and the trucks would bring them to the station. And the station was a cattle station. They were not allowed on the station when all the trains were coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, no, no, no, we would go there and help them out, and get them food, and I don't know, try to make them comfortable. And you try very hard. Your heart is breaking, but you cannot show it. You mentioned that you would try to get yourself out of some deportations. Were you successful at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, one time, I went to the Consul General of Guatemala. And asked him to-- since his money would also be confiscated, why he couldn't take a certain amount of that and buy me. So the money would go to the state of Hamburg, and I wouldn't need to go and tell my mother I  can't go. And it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, before that, I just did it but did it with the Jewish community, or with the Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. But then, they couldn't do anything for me anymore. They said, we cannot do anything. Find another way. So I found one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you and your mother actually deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: July '42. Maybe the 2nd, 3rd, or 4th, around that time, before the 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, her husband, Dr. Rudolphi, my grandparents, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a sister of my grandfather, her daughter. That's all I remember right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened to you. How much advance notice were you given that you were going to be leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 hours, or 36 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you allowed to take?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you can carry, or two suitcases, and that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything special you remember packing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't. All I remember is that it must have been 36 hours because my mother married Dr. Rudolphi, so that would have took a few hours. But I don't know what was packed and who packed what. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mood of you and your family at this time? What did you know about what was going to happen? How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Utter distress, gloom. Though we were told we were going to Terezin and not to Auschwitz, but, in my mind, I was not so sure whether it was true. So we heard that Terezin would be better than Auschwitz. But gloom, being afraid, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all sort of things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like you see there is a dark picture, and you're getting to it, and there is no way away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were glad that you were told you weren't going to Auschwitz. You were hoping not to go there. What, if anything, had you heard about Auschwitz or any other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about Auschwitz, I only heard that people died there and are being killed. Which way they are being killed, I have no idea. I had no idea. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, I didn't want to go without my mother, very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, if anything, had you heard about Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was supposed to be the prominent, quote unquote, 'prominent' camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning that it was not like another concentration camp, but a little different, a little better. So, you know, even that, I had in my imagination, you know, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're given 36 hours' notice. Briefly, you mention your mother getting married. Describe what that was like for you at this time, knowing you were leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew Dr. Rudolphi quite well. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I don't think I thought too much about it while it was going on. Because I thought it was a good idea, but not under those circumstances. But by the same token, I said, it's not for me to decide what is right or wrong at this moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ceremony like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very short ceremony. I know his daughter was one of the witnesses. She was half Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they married by a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who performed the ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody like-- what do you call that here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a justice of the peace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Justice of the peace, something like that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the wedding, describe the rest of that 36 hour period leading up to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We got home, we got our suitcases, and we had to go out there to go to this particular station, with the train awaiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a normal train at the time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with compartments. It was not a cattle train like later, but it was a normal train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a day and a night and a day. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We left, somehow, in the evening, and the next day, and the next day we got there. Not to Terezin, to Bohusovice. I think we ended up to Bohusovice because Theresienstadt didn't have a station, doesn't have a station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went first to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Bohusovice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Bohusovice, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Bohusovice. Bohusovice is a station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt doesn't have a station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, how did you get to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we walked-- endlessly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It may have been Budapest. No, it was an endless, long, long walk. That's how we finally get there, exhausting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of Theresienstadt when you first finally saw it as you were approaching? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the mold and we saw these big buildings. And it was so old. And you couldn't make any rhyme or reason out of it-- none, whatsoever. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It was gloomy. And many people around, and we were just afraid, in a certain way. But then you saw that there are so many people that are walking around who didn't make the impression of being so terribly afraid, which kind of showed me that perhaps it is a place you can maybe survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you-- you get to the camp. Were there Germans supervising you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't see any Germans at that time. Yeah, but Judenaltesten, or whoever, took care of the incoming transports. And we got to-- I don't know which barracks we got into. I really don't know whether it was the Hanover or [NON-ENGLISH] or Hamburg. I have no idea. And it was like a gathering place. And then after a while, we were all dispensed to different little houses, or barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to stay with your mother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my mother and my grandmother for a little while, until I got to the house where the young people were living, the kids. And this is where I lived then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Maybe 17, 16. I don't know, 16, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're at these barracks with the young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. This is a big, big building.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from you were your mother and your grandmother staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and grandmother, maybe about, I should say, not even 10 minutes. One shouldn't forget that between where I lived and between where they lived, the public road from Prague went through, which is very wondrous. Because all these people who were traveling had nothing to do with camp, were traveling from Prague to I don't know where. They all had to go through Theresienstadt. So you always had to wait until no cars were coming, and some black policeman was standing there. And now you can go. Isn't that really odd, eerie?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your day-to-day routine become like at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to work in different things. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was called something [NON-ENGLISH], which means you had to do for 100 hours the dirtiest work they give you to be then entitled to get a little better jobs, or some occupation which is not so tearing on your nerves, or which is not so dirty. But all the newcomers had to do the dirtiest work, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, for you, what did that consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to take care of people who came with transports. And those which were dead and couldn't leave the trains, we had to cart out and close their eyes, which is not very pleasant. And take care of people in an insane asylum, or in a hospital, people are really very sick. Or I mean, things which are really very bad for you, which somehow had nothing to do with the work you did but with the things you saw-- everlasting impressions, which are very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you-- you had to do 100 hours, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: 100 Hours, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take to fulfill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do them. Because my grandfather saw me and took me aside and said that this is not the thing for me to do. I said, well, these are the rules. I cannot bend them. I cannot make new-- I cannot bend them, I cannot make new ones. And he declared that rules can be bent, and ideas can be-- you can have another idea. Think about it. And he was very good in that. And so another girl and I started to think about that. Just the young people should do also heavy work, but should be released from all this very sad kind of emotionally destroying work. And we went to the administration and presented our case. And they agreed to it. They made the provision that we should take care of all the kids, which are from, I don't know, any age until 18, to put them to work. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of 100 hours, you did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: But much less?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much less, much less. Thanks to Grandfather saying, you'd better do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. At the time that you were doing this-- this work, what one image or one thing that you saw stands out most in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of them. You know, that all these people were somebody's grandfather or grandmother. And they probably don't even know where they were. And what can you do? Nothing. The glare with dead eyes, you know, this is something so sad. And I just hoped that I would never see an old person who I once knew. I was glad I didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That kind of is a very-- for a grown-up, enough, on a normal daily type of basis. For a young person, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you managed to get out of this kind of work, what kind of job were you assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I got sick for some reason or another. I had scarlet fever, which is very contagious. So I had to get to a certain kind of hospital and jaundice. I mean, that was-- you know, forget about it. And then, when I got back again, I was very, very weak. And I then worked in the same building as we were living. There was a factory from some people in Prague, and many people worked there. And this is where I was supposed to do flowers out of feathers, very pretty, very pretty things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your specific job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned how to make flowers out of these feathers. I also learned [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] how to make a lot of-- what do you call these? What do you call what you make?

speaker: interviewer
text: What, for the lights?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To make cloth. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Could we cut it for a moment? No?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'll come back to that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. If you can't think of the name, that's OK. It was making some kind of material?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to make material.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. For what purpose? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: For little carpets, and stuff like that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very good with my hands, so I learned more things to do and more things to do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were people who were making greeting cards and just painted those. And that was quite interesting. Then there were people who forged pictures. And that was one of the educations of my life. I don't know where the pictures came from, but the artist absolutely copied anything, whether it was a da Vinci, or whether it was whatever. They copied it into other people, made the exact frames. And this is what they did, so I learned too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made replicas of famous paintings and reproductions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forged them. Forgery-- plain forgery. There was another man, a Dr. Burger, who was an expert in doing miniatures and paint them. And he was an Austrian. And so it was very educational in their own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were living with other children, people who were roughly your age. Was there any one particular person or group of people who you were friends with during this time that helped you through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we all helped each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone you were particularly close with at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. We-- you know, all of us would get the blues, so everybody else would cheer up somebody else. Otherwise, you cannot exist. And you're not a machine, so you get the blues and you get very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes some people will say that, well, of course you have to get the blues. But it's now time that you lose them, because otherwise, it takes your energy away. And by the way, we have to go and get the food now. You know? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is why it's good to have so many people around. In this particular room, I think we were just 9 or 10, so that was very personal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides that, do you remember what other methods you used to try to help each other through or to cheer each other up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We just talked to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What are we going to do when it's over? Where are we going to go? What do we want to see? Things what-- topics you would otherwise not talk about, like what do you think you want to do on your birthday, or the second birthday after the war has ended. You know, so you make plans, which you never realize, but you talk about them, and it makes you want to live for nothing else. I always said, I want to have my birthday under palms. And I want to have the holidays under palms. It took me many years. I had my birthday under palms and the holidays under palms in Barbados, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as food goes at Theresienstadt, what-- you had a daily ration?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a ration of bread, which-- of bread, which we got. I don't know how much. That much, perhaps. And you had that for three days. And sometimes you were so hungry, you wanted to eat it in one day. So then people would go and steal somebody else's bread, or whatever. Or you do a favor for somebody for a little piece of bread. And in the afternoon, as well as in the evening, there were rations. They came, and you came with your little whatever it was, a plate, or whatever you could get your food in-- soup, or vegetables, or whatever. I don't think I ever ate so much horse meat in my life as there. The only time in my life I know I are horse meat, and I never want to eat it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because people talked about it, but meat is meat. And I do remember one of the religious Jewish people said, even to the very kosher ones, you have to eat the meat. Rules do not apply anymore. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they were not plentiful. But you wouldn't starve from them. Really, you wouldn't. It just keeps you alive, vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were you able to see your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening-- until curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was everyone holding up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody gave somebody else a pep talk, whether you believed it or not, but you did. You put up a front. And when you talk aloud long enough, you believe it yourself. And you help another person, you know, over a very bad time. Because that happens, of course. You got claustrophobic. And somebody else got sick, and somebody else died. Don't forget that, oftentimes, 1,000 people came a day and 1,000 people died. It evened out. So you had all the corpses lying around. They threw some-- a sheet over them, but you know so-and-so were lying there, until they came to pick them up. So you really got used to that. And it's hard. It was hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who in your family do you think had the hardest time at Theresienstadt? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And why?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's husband, Dr. Rudolphi. Because he couldn't believe that his daughter wouldn't send him more parcels. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that kind of disturbed him completely. He had figured that she would see to it that he had enough food. But, unfortunately, she didn't. And my mother had a very hard time, because her father died, and her mother died, and her aunt died, and her cousin died. So this was very, very hard on my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your grandparents, they both died at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandpa died, I think, in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '43. And-- and Grandmother, a few months later. And he had been-- he had been sick, and I had been removed from him. And he was not in a hospital, but in one of those sick rooms. I can't explain that. And when I heard that I had scarlet fever, and I had to run, and I had to be in isolation, I knew I had to cross the street with all these cars going back and forth to Prague, that I had to see him before. Because I knew he might die. And I went to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, I have one eye, but I can see you have scarlet fever. What are you doing here? And I told him that I would be away for six weeks, and I was afraid I wouldn't see him anymore. And he said, no, I wouldn't. Have a kiss goodbye, and I left. Do you know what he was sick with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was something in his eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any last words or advice he gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll have to end tape three here. We'll pick this up with tape number 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
